name: mcp_forth
summary: Liam Howatt's mcp_forth (2024): an embeddable native-codegen Forth targeting x86-32, Xtensa (ESP32-S3), and a portable bytecode VM, designed to *self-compile inside* MCUs with 100 KB–10 MB RAM. Unusual because most embeddable language runtimes pick one of those design points; mcp_forth covers the spectrum.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Liam Howatt's **mcp_forth** (2024) is an embeddable native-codegen Forth targeting x86-32, Xtensa (ESP32-S3), and a portable bytecode VM, designed to *self-compile inside* MCUs with 100 KB–10 MB RAM and to drive peripherals as small as 8 KB.

The architecture is unusual because most embeddable language runtimes pick one of those points (interpreter on tiny MCUs, native codegen on large ones); mcp_forth covers the spectrum with a shared front-end and pluggable back-ends.

sections.title: Design position
sections.content:
The specific value for new-language design is the existence proof: "compile inside an ESP32-S3" is achievable with a small enough compiler core, even one that is not extracted from a larger toolchain. Sits next to Mecrisp and zeptoforth as a third design point in the embedded Forth space — multi-backend rather than single-target.

The cost is the inverse of Mecrisp's: no single backend gets the depth of optimisation a single-target Forth can deliver, and per-target maturity varies.
